page_title: Fallow — Smart Daily Time Allocation & To-Do Planner
page_meta_description: A smart to-do planner that splits your daily time across tasks with the Zenith Gradient algorithm — balancing effort, enjoyment, and flow to maximize output.
seo_image_alt: Fallow — a to-do app that finds the mathematically optimal way to spend your day
about_hook_title: A to-do app that does calculus so you don't have to
about_hook_body: Every productive day poses the same question: you have more tasks than hours — how should you split your time to get the most out of the day? Fallow doesn't guess and doesn't split evenly. It models each task's productivity curve and solves for the time allocation that maximizes your total output, within the hours in your day and the mental and physical energy you can actually sustain.
about_how_body_1: Productivity over time isn't flat. On any task you start at some initial level, ramp up as you get into the zone, peak at flow state, then decay as you tire. Fallow describes that curve with three things you feel intuitively: how demanding the task is (effort), how much you like doing it (enjoyment), and how long it takes you to get going (time to flow).
about_how_body_2: Enjoyable, low-effort tasks start productive right away; hard, unpleasant ones start slow but peak higher. Every task also has a mathematically optimal stopping point — roughly 1.5 to 1.79 times its time-to-flow — beyond which your average productivity on it falls. Fallow plans in 15-minute blocks and never pushes a task past that point.
about_different_body_1: Fallow solves the allocation exactly: greedy marginal analysis over time-block values, with an exhaustive search over which tasks deserve any time at all once context-switch costs are charged. It then reports the Fallow Gain — how much better the optimized plan is than an equal split.
about_different_body_2: A dual energy-pool model tracks cognitive and physical capacity separately, so the plan alternates hard thinking with physical work instead of burning one system down. The model also personalizes itself: log how long it took you to reach flow, and a least-squares fit tunes its constants to you.
about_energy_post: takes it further — breaks and quitting time emerge from the math.
about_faq_a1: Yes. Fallow is free and open source — the full model, including every formula and derivation, is documented publicly.
about_faq_a1_support: If it helps you, you can support development and
about_faq_a2: Entirely on your device, in your browser's local database (IndexedDB). Your tasks never leave your machine and no account is required.
about_faq_q3: What is the Zenith Gradient Algorithm?
about_faq_a3: An approach to daily time allocation that models each task's productivity as a curve that rises to flow state and then decays, and allocates your day by marginal value — every next block of time goes to the task where it currently counts most. It originated in the article "How to Over-Engineer a To-Do App"; Fallow extends it with corrected math, energy pools, and context-switch costs.
header_tagline: Splits your daily time budget so each extra minute goes to the task where it counts most — accounting for flow ramp-up, context-switch costs, and your cognitive and physical capacity, and never pushing a task past its point of diminishing returns.
list_empty_hint: Add a task above to begin tracking
task_run_order_tooltip: Suggested run order — alternates cognitive and physical work so the resting energy system recovers
task_nature_cognitive_description: Cognitive task — mental difficulty dominates, draws on the cognitive capacity pool
task_nature_physical_description: Physical task — physical difficulty dominates, draws on the physical capacity pool
task_nature_hybrid_description: Hybrid task — balanced mental and physical demands, draws on both capacity pools
task_inputs_tooltip: Your slider inputs: Physical difficulty, Mental difficulty, Enjoyment — hover the task and use ✎ to change them
task_derived_tooltip: What the Fallow model derived from your sliders: true effort E (1–5), time to reach flow state ϕ, and the optimal stopping time (1.79×ϕ) beyond which average productivity declines
task_flow_badge_tooltip: Measured minutes-to-flow you logged for this task — data points like this personalize the model's c₁,c₂,c₃ constants (least-squares fit)
task_allocation_tooltip: Suggested time allocation for today, and the task's priority score (intrinsic value × 10) that drives it
task_log_flow_tooltip: Log how many minutes it took to get into flow — measured data points personalize the model's c₁,c₂,c₃ constants
budget_unplanned: {hours}h left unplanned — capped by optimal stopping times & capacity pools
budget_unplanned_title: The plan deliberately stops short of your budget: each task is capped at its optimal stopping time (1.79×ϕ, where average productivity peaks) and by your cognitive/physical capacity pools. The unused hours are recovery time, not lost time.
budget_cognitive_hint: Intense mental work you sustain per day (~4h)
budget_physical_hint: Intense physical work you sustain per day (~6h)
budget_model_tooltip: The model's flow-state constants (c₁, c₂, c₃) are a ridge least-squares fit of your measured time-to-flow data, anchored to the article's defaults. Click to manage the logged data points.
budget_delete_log_title: Remove this data point — the model refits without it
budget_reset_confirm: Delete all {count} logs and revert to defaults?
budget_reset_title: Delete all flow logs — the constants are always derived from the logs (never stored), so the model reverts to the article defaults
momentum_tooltip: Average enjoyment minus difficulty across your remaining tasks — completed ones drop out. Upward = the work ahead is motivating, Reset Reqd = it drains more than it rewards.
model_status_personalized: Model personalized from {count} time-to-flow logs (⚡) — blended with the defaults, sharpens as you log more
model_status_personalized_one: Model personalized from 1 time-to-flow log (⚡) — blended with the defaults, sharpens as you log more
model_status_implausible: Your {count} flow logs produced an implausible fit (predicted flow times over 16h) — using default constants. Check the logs below for mistakes.
model_status_implausible_one: Your 1 flow log produced an implausible fit (predicted flow times over 16h) — using default constants. Check the logs below for mistakes.
model_status_default: Model uses default constants — log time-to-flow (⚡) on tasks to start personalizing
banner_future_body: tasks and budget you set here are saved to {date} — open it on that day to work the plan.
banner_past_body: you can still check tasks on or off (forgot one before midnight? fix it here — changes are saved), but adding or editing tasks is only possible on today.
metric_zenith_gain_desc: Productivity improvement from Fallow optimization vs. a naive equal time split. Saturates at +999% — with many tasks and few hours the naive plan achieves almost nothing.
metric_yield_index_desc: Did you complete the highest-priority tasks? Compares your completed work against the optimal selection.
metric_completion_rate_desc: Priority-weighted progress. High-value tasks contribute more to this percentage than low-priority ones.
metric_flow_coverage_desc: Tasks that reach flow state (allocated time ≥ ϕ). Low coverage means too many tasks for budget — drop tasks or add hours.
metric_human_capacity_desc: {type} limit ({hours}h/day, configurable in Time Budget). The allocator enforces these pools, so suggested plans stay ≤100% — this shows how much of your sustainable capacity the plan uses.
metric_time_scarcity_desc: How stretched is your time budget? Higher values mean too many tasks for the hours available.
metric_bottleneck_desc: Among your remaining tasks, the one with the highest effort-to-enjoyability ratio in Fallow terms — the biggest drag on your list, even if today's plan gave it no time.
metric_burnout_risk_desc: Your planned day simulated through the energy model: each task drains the cognitive and physical reservoirs by its demand and duration (drain and recovery rates come from your own calibration logs). Risk is how depleted the most-drained reservoir ends the day — hours you intend beyond the plan are simulated as extra work.
metric_cognitive_load_desc: Percentage of your day allocated to mental/cognitive tasks.
metric_physical_load_desc: Percentage of your day allocated to physical tasks.
metric_energy_balance_desc: Distribution of Cognitive vs physical work. Alternating types extends total productive hours.
metric_schedule_integrity_desc: Share of the plan's committed time that is productive work rather than context-switch overhead. One long session ≈ 100%; many small sessions drive it down.
metric_friction_index_desc: Share of your allocated time spent on work that is harder than it is enjoyable (time-weighted difficulty−enjoyment gap on the 1–10 scales). 100% = every planned hour is difficulty-10, enjoyment-1 work.
metric_deep_work_desc: Percentage of time allocated to high mental difficulty (≥7) tasks requiring sustained focus.
metric_quick_wins_desc: Count of easy, enjoyable tasks available for momentum building and motivation boosts.
metric_task_variety_desc: Diversity across mental/physical spectrum. Mixing cognitive, physical, and balanced tasks prevents fatigue.
metric_grind_density_desc: Percentage of tasks where difficulty exceeds enjoyment. High values signal willpower drain.
metric_sustainable_work_desc: Percentage of time on tasks where enjoyment ≥ difficulty. Higher = more energizing workday.
metric_recovery_ratio_desc: Easy tasks (≤4) per hard tasks (≥7). Aim for 1:2 or 1:3 ratio for ultradian rhythm recovery.
metric_day_profile_desc: Your day's character based on average difficulty and enjoyment. Flow Zone = challenging and engaging, Grind Mode = demanding work, Cruise = light and enjoyable, Routine = low-key tasks.
metric_avg_physical_desc: Average physical difficulty across all tasks.
metric_avg_mental_desc: Average mental/cognitive difficulty across all tasks.
metric_avg_enjoyment_desc: Average enjoyment across all tasks. Higher values indicate more engaging work.
cal_meta_description: Month and week overview of your Fallow days.
cal_empty_1: No data in this {view} yet — days you plan on the
ana_meta_description: Completion trends and task stats across your Fallow days.
ana_subtitle: How your days have gone — completion, streaks, and day profiles over time.
ana_empty: Nothing to analyze in this range yet.
ana_empty_hint_2: page and it will show up here.
ana_with_completion: {count} with at least one task done
ana_streak_note: consecutive days with a completed task
ana_chart_hint_day: Priority-weighted completion rate per day — hover a bar for details
ana_chart_hint_year: Monthly average of the priority-weighted daily completion rate
ana_chart_aria: Bar chart of completion rate over the {range}
ana_day_profiles_hint: Character of your active days: challenging & engaging (Flow), demanding (Grind), light & fun (Cruise), or low-key (Routine)
energy_meta_description: Experimental total-output scheduler: energy reservoirs drain as you work and recover as you rest; breaks and quitting time emerge from the math.
energy_intro_1: A different engine than the main page: instead of maximizing average productivity, this scheduler maximizes
energy_intro_2: across your day. Two energy reservoirs (cognitive and physical) drain while you work and recover while you rest, and warm-up restarts every time you switch — so rest breaks, task order, and your quitting time all
energy_intro_3: instead of being rules. It shares the task list with the main page — edits here save to today's session.
energy_no_open_tasks_hint: Add one below — the Energy Lab schedules the same task list as the main page.
energy_all_done_hint: The planner needs an open task — un-check one below or add a new one.
energy_set_window: Set a day window above 0 hours.
energy_chart_aria: Energy levels and output rate over the day
energy_drag_hint: Drag a slider and watch the schedule above re-optimize. Edits save to today's session.
energy_nothing_scheduled: Nothing scheduled — with the current parameters, free time is worth more than any task's output. Lower the free-time value or check your day window.
energy_vs_classic: Output vs the classic plan, judged by this model
energy_reset_defaults_title: Restore all model parameters to their defaults
energy_day_window_hint: Follows today's budget until you change it; changes stay in the lab
energy_cognitive_drain_hint: How fast deep mental work empties the tank
energy_recovery_rate_hint: How fast rest refills the reservoirs
energy_free_time_value_hint: What an hour NOT worked is worth to you, in output units — this is what makes the plan stop instead of filling the whole day
energy_evening_energy_hint: How much ending the day fresh (vs wrecked) is worth
energy_satiety_hint: Diminishing returns for piling more of the day onto the same task — after this many good sessions' worth of output, extra output on it counts half. 0 turns it off (one task can then win the whole day)
energy_micro_recovery_hint: Share of recovery that stays active even while working flat out (micro-pauses between efforts) — keeps a maximum-demand task above a small energy floor instead of draining toward zero. 0 turns it off
energy_log_drain_tooltip: After working on this task, rate how drained you feel (mind and body, 0–10) — the ratings calibrate the drain rates to you. Applying a fit re-plans the whole window from scratch, so the payoff lands on your next session, not the rest of today
energy_drain_mind_title: How mentally drained you feel now: 0 = fresh, 10 = completely spent
energy_drain_body_title: How physically drained you feel now: 0 = fresh, 10 = completely spent
energy_calibration_hint: Fits the two drain rates to your 🪫 end-of-session ratings (anchored to the defaults — every rating nudges them further). The fit conditions on the current recovery settings — calibrate those first from ☕ rest pairs below.
energy_calibration_empty: No ratings yet. After a session on a task, hit 🪫 on it and rate how drained your mind and body feel — a few ratings personalize the drain rates.
energy_apply_fit_title: Set the two drain-rate parameters to the fitted values
energy_delete_drain_log_title: Remove this rating from the calibration
energy_reset_drain_title: Delete every drain rating — the calibration reverts to the defaults
energy_recovery_calibration_hint: Fits the recovery rate to your ☕ rest pairs — rate mind and body before and after a break. During rest, drain fades independently of the drain rates, so a few pairs pin down recovery on its own; the drain calibration then builds on it.
energy_recovery_calibration_empty: No rest pairs yet. Before a break, note how drained mind and body feel (0–10); rate them again afterwards — a few pairs personalize the recovery rate. If you recover fast, short breaks carry the most signal.
energy_apply_recovery_fit_title: Set the recovery-rate parameter to the fitted value
energy_stop_calibration_hint: Fits the free-time value to when you actually stop working: each past day's 🪫 worked minutes are compared against that day's window — stopping early means leisure is worth more to you than the model assumed. Calibrate recovery and drain first; this fit conditions on them.
energy_stop_calibration_empty: No finished days yet. Log 🪫 drain ratings (with worked minutes) on your tasks; every past day with ratings becomes one stop observation here.
energy_stop_calibration_censored: No informative days yet — days worked to the window's edge (or barely at all), and days that look cut short mid-session (an interruption, not a chosen stop), reveal only an inequality, not an indifference point.
energy_apply_stop_fit_title: Set the free-time value parameter to the fitted value
energy_delete_rest_log_title: Remove this pair from the calibration
energy_reset_rest_title: Delete every rest pair — the calibration reverts to the defaults
data_delete_confirm: Delete all data? This wipes every task, routine, and observation and cannot be undone.
backup_import_failed: Import failed — this doesn't look like a Fallow backup file.
privacy_controller_body: Responsible for the data processing on this website (full contact details in the imprint):
privacy_local_heading: Your data stays in your browser
privacy_local_body: Everything you enter into Fallow — tasks, daily sessions, ratings, calibration data and settings — is stored exclusively on your own device (IndexedDB and localStorage in your browser). It is never transmitted to us or to any server, and there are no user accounts. If you clear your browser data, it is gone. This local storage is strictly necessary to provide the app and therefore requires no consent (§ 25 (2) TDDDG).
privacy_hosting_body: This website is hosted by Vercel Inc., 440 N Barranca Ave #4133, Covina, CA 91723, USA. When you visit it, Vercel processes the data your browser transmits with each request — in particular your IP address, date and time, the requested page, browser and operating system — in server logs to deliver the site and to ensure its stability and security. The legal basis is our legitimate interest in the reliable operation of the site (Art. 6 (1) (f) GDPR).
privacy_analytics_body: We use Vercel Web Analytics to measure how this site is used in aggregate (page views, referrer, browser and device type, country/region derived from the IP address). The tool sets no cookies and stores nothing on your device. Visitors are counted via a hash derived from the incoming request; it is automatically discarded after 24 hours and cannot track you across other websites. The legal basis is our legitimate interest in privacy-friendly reach measurement (Art. 6 (1) (f) GDPR).
privacy_speed_body: We also use Vercel Speed Insights to collect anonymized loading-performance metrics (Core Web Vitals) for visited pages. It sets no cookies and builds no visitor profiles. The legal basis is our legitimate interest in keeping the site fast and working (Art. 6 (1) (f) GDPR).
privacy_transfer_body: Vercel Inc. is based in the USA. Vercel is certified under the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (DPF); in addition, Vercel's data processing agreement, which includes the EU Standard Contractual Clauses, applies. An adequate level of data protection is therefore ensured for these transfers (Art. 45, 46 GDPR).
privacy_rights_body: You have the right of access (Art. 15 GDPR) and the rights to rectification (Art. 16), erasure (Art. 17), restriction of processing (Art. 18), data portability (Art. 20) and — for processing based on legitimate interest — objection (Art. 21 GDPR). You also have the right to lodge a complaint with a data protection supervisory authority. To exercise your rights, use the contact details in the imprint.
error_404_body: This page doesn't exist. Your tasks are safe — they live in your browser.
error_body: An unexpected error occurred. Reloading usually fixes it — your data is stored locally and is not affected.
